children.text: Pleadings
What Are Pleadings? - Parties' initial statements filed before full discovery; parties don't have complete facts when pleading
Types: Complaint (Plaintiff, Rule 8(a)) and Answer (Defendant, Rules 8(b)-(c), 13)
Historical Evolution: Common Law (hypertechnical) → Code Pleading (facts) → Notice Pleading (1938, give notice) → Plausibility Pleading (2007-2009, plausible facts)
Complaint Requirements - Rule 8(a)
8(a)(1): Short and plain statement of grounds for jurisdiction
8(a)(2): Short and plain statement of claim showing entitlement to relief - where pleading standards apply
8(a)(3): Demand for relief (prayer for judgment) - compensatory, punitive, injunctive, declaratory, attorney's fees, costs
Rule 8(d) - Pleading Flexibility
8(d)(1): Simple, concise, direct; no technical forms required
8(d)(2): Alternative/hypothetical claims permitted
8(d)(3): Inconsistent claims permitted (e.g., 'contract invalid' AND 'breach of valid contract')
Rule 8(e) - Construction: Pleadings 'must be construed so as to do justice'
Rule 10(a) - Caption Requirements: Court name, case number (assigned by clerk), parties' names, document title
Rule 7(a) - Types of Pleadings Allowed: Designates which documents qualify as 'pleadings' (complaint, answer, reply to counterclaim, etc.)
Rule 10(b) - Form of Pleadings: Numbered paragraphs, each limited to single set of circumstances, separate counts for separate claims
Answer - Rule 8(b) & (c)
Admissions & Denials - Rule 8(b): Admit, Deny, or Lack of Knowledge (functions as denial). Failure to deny = admission
Rule 8(b)(4) - Partial Denial: If denying only part of an allegation, must specifically state what is true and deny only the rest
Rule 8(b)(5) - Three Response Options: (1) Admit (treated as true), (2) Deny (issue for trial), (3) Lack of knowledge/information (functions as denial). Failure to deny = admission
Affirmative Defenses - Rule 8(c): Concedes claim arguendo but asserts additional facts defeating liability. Must be pleaded or WAIVED (Ingraham v. United States - can't 'lie behind a log')
Examples: Statute of limitations, release, comparative negligence, waiver, estoppel, laches, fraud, failure of consideration, unclean hands

Reis Robotics USA v. Concept Industries: Motion to strike insufficient 'affirmative defenses.' Issues: (1) defenses that are really denials (not affirmative defenses), (2) vague laundry-list defenses ('laches, waiver, estoppel...') too general, (3) 'reserving right to add defenses' not allowed - must plead now or waive

Distinguishing Denials from Affirmative Defenses — 'Never went on property' = denial (contradicts plaintiff's allegation). 'Had permission' = affirmative defense (admits entry, asserts additional fact defeating liability). 'Adverse possession' = affirmative defense. 'Neighbor was negligent' = counterclaim. Key test: if defendant bears burden of proof → affirmative defense
Counterclaims - Rule 13
13(a) Compulsory: MUST assert or waived - arises from same transaction/occurrence
13(b) Permissive: MAY assert - any claim not from same transaction/occurrence; can bring later
13(c) Relief: Counterclaim relief can exceed or differ from plaintiff's requested relief

King v. Blanton: Equitable waiver of compulsory counterclaims - even if first case settled (not adjudicated), may still bar later lawsuit. Considers judicial efficiency, prejudice, party's knowledge/conduct, opportunity to protect via settlement negotiations
Leftover Rule 12(b) defenses not yet waived
Rule 9(b) - Heightened Pleading for Fraud: Must allege with specificity when, where, and how fraud was committed. Malice/intent/knowledge may be alleged generally
Leatherman v. Tarrant County (1993): Courts cannot extend heightened pleading to categories not listed in Rule 9(b)
Pleading Standards
Notice Pleading Era (1938-2007)
Conley v. Gibson (1957): Complaint sufficient unless 'beyond doubt plaintiff can prove no set of facts' - low threshold
Dioguardi v. Durning (2d Cir. 1944): Pro se barely comprehensible complaint sufficient - court identifies applicable law from alleged facts
Characteristics: Low bar, all allegations taken as true, court identifies legal theories, screening shifted to discovery/SJ
Plausibility Pleading Era (2007-Present) - Twombly-Iqbal

Bell Atlantic v. Twombly (2007): Antitrust case - parallel conduct equally consistent with conspiracy or lawful behavior. 'No set of facts' standard retired. Must plead facts making claim 'plausible on its face'
Ashcroft v. Iqbal (2009): Applies Twombly to ALL civil actions, not just antitrust. Based on Rule 8 which governs all civil cases
Two-Step Analysis
Step 1: Identify and discard conclusory allegations - 'threadbare recitals of elements' and 'formulaic recitations' get NO presumption of truth
Step 2: Assess whether remaining well-pleaded facts plausibly state a claim. More than possible/conceivable, less than probable. Context-specific using 'judicial experience and common sense'

Swanson v. Citibank (7th Cir. 2010): Majority (Wood) - plausibility does not equal probability; ask 'could this have happened,' not 'did it happen.' Posner dissent - strict Iqbal reading requiring dismissal when obvious alternative explanation exists
Competing Inferences Problem: When facts equally support innocent and guilty explanations, plaintiff must plead something more to make liability more plausible
Rule 12(b)(6) Motion to Dismiss
Purpose: Attack legal sufficiency - the 'So what?' motion. Used when not legally sufficient or not factually sufficient
Court assumes all factual allegations TRUE; does NOT weigh evidence or resolve disputes. If court considers matters outside pleadings → converts to Rule 56 SJ (Rule 12(d))
Does NOT decide whether plaintiff will win, resolve factual disputes, or require plaintiff to prove case. Dismissal usually with leave to amend

Policy Implications: Favors defendants (dismiss before expensive discovery, info asymmetry). Disadvantages plaintiffs (Catch-22: need facts to plead but facts in defendant's control, only obtainable through discovery)

Catch-22 Problem — Plaintiffs need discovery to plead plausibly, but can't get discovery until they survive motion to dismiss. Information asymmetry favors defendants who control the facts. Discovery doesn't begin until after 12(b)(6) is resolved

Ohio vs. Federal Distinction — Ohio state courts still use notice pleading (short and plain statement, give fair notice). Federal courts use Twombly-Iqbal plausibility pleading. Key exam distinction: same complaint may survive in Ohio but fail in federal court

Exam Strategy: Two-Layer Analysis — Layer 1: Apply strict Twombly-Iqbal two-step (strip conclusory allegations, assess plausibility). Layer 2: Acknowledge Swanson's softening ('could this have happened?' not 'did it happen?'). Argue both ways to show doctrinal sophistication
Rule 11 - Care and Candor
Overview: Regulates attorney conduct, requires 'Stop and Think' before signing/filing/submitting/advocating. Standard: 'inquiry reasonable under the circumstances'
Rule 11(a) - Signature: At least one attorney of record must sign. Court must strike unsigned paper unless promptly corrected
Rule 11(b) - Four Certifications
11(b)(1): No improper purpose (harassment, delay, needless cost increase)
11(b)(2): Legal contentions warranted by existing law OR nonfrivolous argument for extending/modifying/reversing/establishing new law
11(b)(3): Factual contentions have evidentiary support OR if specifically identified, will likely have support after reasonable investigation/discovery
11(b)(4): Denials warranted on evidence OR reasonably based on belief/lack of info (primarily applies to defendant's answer)
Key Cases

Hays v. Sony Corp.: Attorney Guyon sanctioned $14,895 for filing common law copyright claim (abolished 1976), seeking unavailable remedies, and doing NO pre-filing investigation. Objective negligence standard - good faith is NO defense

Hunter v. Earthgrains: Sanctions VACATED - arguing against controlling precedent (Austin) was non-frivolous because circuit split existed, Supreme Court authority supported her, and attorneys are entitled to argue for change. Losing does NOT equal Rule 11 violation

Blue v. U.S. Dept. of Army: Chief Judge Wilkinson warned Rule 11 must not chill advocacy for legal change. 'If arguing against precedent were forbidden, the parties who brought Brown v. Board of Education might have been thought to have engaged in sanctionable conduct for pursuing claims in the face of Plessy v. Ferguson.'

Austin v. Owens-Brockway (4th Cir. 1996): Controlling precedent that general CBA arbitration clauses DO apply to statutory discrimination claims. Was the precedent Hunter argued against in Hunter v. Earthgrains

Wright v. Universal Maritime Service Corp. (1998, SCOTUS): Supreme Court validated Hunter's legal theory after she filed but before sanctions were imposed. Demonstrates that arguing against controlling precedent can be vindicated

Szabo Food Service — Amount of pre-filing investigation depends on (1) time available and (2) probability more investigation will turn up important evidence. Rule 11 does NOT require steps that are not cost-justified. Cost-benefit analysis for investigation

National Objective Standard — No locality rule (unlike medical malpractice). If you lack expertise, must either associate with a specialist OR 'bone up on the relevant law at every step' and 'litigate very carefully, just as a new driver must drive very carefully'

En Banc Review as Pathway — Arguing against controlling circuit precedent is non-frivolous because en banc review exists as a legitimate mechanism for changing circuit law. Normal process: 3-judge panel → en banc (all judges) → Supreme Court. Only full court can overrule its own precedent

Sources for 'Nonfrivolous Argument for Change' under 11(b)(2) — Circuit splits, Supreme Court dicta, dissenting opinions, law review articles, scholarly commentary, trends in other jurisdictions. Losing does NOT equal frivolous; argument needs legitimate basis, not ultimate success

Connection to Ohio Professional Conduct Rules — Rule 1.1 (Competence: legal knowledge, skill, thoroughness reasonably necessary), Rule 1.2 (Diligence: reasonable diligence and promptness), Rule 3.1 (Meritorious Claims: no frivolous claims, but good faith argument for change allowed). Parallel Rule 11 obligations; violation of Rule 11 may also be professional responsibility violation
Rule 11(c) - Sanctions
Who: Attorneys, law firms, parties. CANNOT impose monetary sanctions on represented party for 11(b)(2) legal basis violations
11(c)(1): Can sanction anyone who 'presents' a document (signing, filing, submitting, advocating). Law firms jointly responsible absent exceptional circumstances
11(c)(4) Types of Sanctions: Nonmonetary directives (reprimand, CLE), penalty paid to court, attorney's fees to movant (only if motion-based, not sua sponte). Must be 'sufficient to deter' - no more
11(c)(5): Cannot impose monetary sanctions on represented party for 11(b)(2) violations (bad legal arguments = lawyer's responsibility only). Sanctions focused on deterrence, not compensation
Types: Nonmonetary directives (reprimand, CLE), penalty to court, attorney's fees (only if motion-based). Focus: deterrence, not compensation
Pathway 1 - Motion by Opposing Party (Rule 11(c)(2)): Serve motion → 21-day safe harbor to withdraw/correct → if not fixed, file with court
Pathway 2 - Sua Sponte (Rule 11(c)(3)): Court issues show cause order. NO safe harbor. Cannot award attorney's fees. Reserved for conduct 'akin to contempt'
Rule 11(d): Does NOT apply to discovery (Rule 37 governs discovery sanctions). Oral arguments also NOT subject to Rule 11
Defendant's Response
Three Options: File Answer (Rule 8(b),(c)), File Motion to Dismiss (Rule 12(b)), or Do Nothing → Default (Rule 55). Must respond within 21 days (or 60 if waiver of service)
Rule 12(a)(1) - Timing: Must file answer or Rule 12 motion within 21 days after service. Exception: 60 days if defendant timely returns waiver of service
Default and Default Judgment - Rule 55
Step 1 - Entry of Default (55(a)): Clerk enters when defendant fails to respond within 21 days
Step 2 - Default Judgment (55(b)): By clerk (limited) or court (on motion). Setting aside: different standards for 55(a) vs 55(b)

Rule 55(c) - Setting Aside Default: Different standards apply depending on stage. Setting aside entry of default (55(a)) = lower standard ('good cause'). Setting aside default judgment (55(b)) = higher standard (must meet Rule 60(b) requirements)
Pre-Answer Motions - Rule 12(b)
12(b)(1) Lack of subject matter jurisdiction
12(b)(2) Lack of personal jurisdiction
12(b)(3) Improper venue
12(b)(4) Insufficient process
12(b)(5) Insufficient service of process
12(b)(6) Failure to state a claim (most common)
12(b)(7) Failure to join party under Rule 19
Other Rule 12 Motions
Rule 12(e) - More Definite Statement: When complaint is 'hopelessly vague' and cannot reasonably prepare response. Rarely granted
Rule 12(f) - Motion to Strike: Remove inflammatory, improper, or irrelevant material. Not a substitute for 12(b)(6)

Matos v. Nextran: Illustrates Rules 12(e) and 12(f). 12(e) issue: complaint incorporated prior paragraphs by reference without separating warranty claims. 12(f) issue: strike references to 'illegal conduct' and 'all damages of every kind' as inflammatory/improper
The Waiver Trap - CRITICAL CONCEPT
Omnibus Motion Rule - 12(g)(2): Must consolidate ALL available Rule 12 defenses in ONE motion (first motion)
WAIVABLE - Rule 12(h)(1): 12(b)(2)-(5), 12(e), 12(f) - waived if not in first Rule 12 motion OR answer
Can Raise Later - Rule 12(h)(2): 12(b)(6) failure to state claim, 12(b)(7) failure to join party - can raise through trial
NEVER Waived - Rule 12(h)(3): 12(b)(1) subject matter jurisdiction - can be raised at ANY time, even on appeal

Hunter v. Serv-Tech, Inc.: Defendant filed 12(b)(5) motion, then tried to raise 12(b)(2) later. Court held 12(b)(2) waived by not including in first motion under 12(g)(2). Expressly 'reserving rights' in motion does NOT prevent waiver
Amendments to Pleadings - Rule 15

General Principle: Liberal amendment permitted. Pleadings filed before full discovery; should reflect educated understanding after investigation. Applies to both plaintiff (complaint) and defendant (answer)
Amendments Before Trial - Rule 15(a)
As Matter of Course - 15(a)(1): ONE TIME ONLY without permission. Within 21 days after serving OR within 21 days after responsive pleading/12(b),(e),(f) motion
Other Amendments - 15(a)(2): Need written consent of opposing party OR court's leave. Standard: 'freely give leave when justice so requires'
Foman v. Davis (1962) factors: Undue delay, bad faith/dilatory motive, repeated failure to cure, undue prejudice, futility of amendment

Beeck v. Aqua Slide 'N' Dive: Liberal standard for granting leave to amend. Focus on prejudice (preparation prejudice, not merits) and futility (e.g., SOL run, fails to state claim). Reinforces Foman v. Davis 'freely give leave' approach
Amendments During/After Trial - Rule 15(b)
15(b)(1) Based on Objection: Court should freely permit if aids merits and no prejudice shown
15(b)(2) Issues Tried by Consent: Issue not in pleadings but tried with express/implied consent treated as if raised in pleadings
Relation Back - Rule 15(c)
Purpose: Allows amendments after statute of limitations has run by 'relating back' to original filing date
15(c)(1)(A): Applicable law allows relation back
State law may be more generous than federal relation-back rules. Court applies whichever law (state or federal) provides relation back - acts as a one-way ratchet favoring the amending party
15(c)(1)(B) - New Claim, Same Party: Amendment arises from same conduct/transaction/occurrence as original pleading
Bonerb v. Richard J. Caron Foundation: New claims related to original facts can relate back; defendant already on notice to preserve evidence
15(c)(1)(C) - New/Corrected Defendant: ALL requirements must be met
(i) Same transaction/occurrence (15(c)(1)(B) satisfied)
(ii) Within 90 days (Rule 4(m)): New defendant received notice AND knew/should have known action would have been brought against it but for mistake in identity
Krupski v. Costa Crociere (2010, SCOTUS): 'Mistake' inquiry focuses on what DEFENDANT knew, not plaintiff's diligence. Shared counsel = notice. Amendment allowed
'Mistake' = about WHO you meant to sue (misnomer, wrong entity), NOT discovering a new wrongdoer
Discovery - Rules 26-37
Scope of Discovery - Rule 26(b)(1)
Three-Part Test: (1) Not privileged, (2) Relevant to claim/defense, (3) Proportional to needs of case. ALL must be met. Discoverable ≠ Admissible

Proportionality - 6 Factors (2015 amendment): Importance of issues, amount in controversy, parties' relative access to info, parties' resources, importance of discovery in resolving issues, burden/expense vs. likely benefit
Limits: Privilege (26(b)(1)), irrelevance, disproportionality, cumulative/duplicative (26(b)(2)(C)), work product (26(b)(3)), expert limits (26(b)(4)), protective orders (26(c))
Rule 1 - Foundational Purpose: 'Just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action.' Underlies all discovery proportionality analysis and guides interpretation of discovery rules
Privileges - Discovery 'Full Stop'
Privileged info = discovery ends. Relevance and proportionality do NOT override valid privilege
Types: Attorney-client, spousal, doctor-patient, psychotherapist-patient (Jaffee v. Redmond), 5th Amendment, clergy-penitent
Privilege protects COMMUNICATIONS, not underlying FACTS. Must affirmatively assert + provide privilege log (Rule 26(b)(5)(A))
Work Product Doctrine - Rule 26(b)(3)
Foundation: Hickman v. Taylor (1947) - if attorneys must hand over everything, they stop writing things down; harms clients and justice

Six Elements: (1) Document/tangible thing, (2) Prepared in anticipation of litigation (BIGGEST BATTLEGROUND), (3) By/for party or representative, (4) Otherwise discoverable, (5) Substantial need + no equivalent, (6) Must protect opinion work product
'Anticipation of Litigation' approaches: Specific Claim (narrowest), Primary Purpose (majority), Ad Hoc/totality (broadest, In re Sealed Case). Key issue: dual-purpose documents

Two Tiers: Fact Work Product (qualified - can overcome with substantial need + no equivalent) vs. Opinion Work Product (near-absolute - mental impressions, conclusions, opinions, legal theories virtually never discoverable)
Rule 26(b)(3)(C): Any person can obtain their OWN prior statement without any special showing

Rule 26(b)(3)(A) - Fact/Ordinary Work Product: Qualified protection only. Can be overcome if requesting party shows (1) substantial need for the materials AND (2) cannot obtain substantial equivalent without undue hardship
Rule 26(b)(3)(B) - Opinion Work Product: Near-absolute protection for mental impressions, conclusions, opinions, and legal theories of attorneys. Virtually never discoverable regardless of need

Coastal States Gas Corp.: Narrowest 'specific claim' test for anticipation of litigation - requires a specific claim supported by concrete facts that make litigation likely. Contrasts with broader 'primary purpose' (majority) and 'ad hoc/totality' (In re Sealed Case) approaches
Discovery Tools

Mandatory Initial Disclosures - Rule 26(a)(1): Automatic exchange within 14 days after 26(f) conference. Witnesses, documents/ESI, damages computation, insurance agreements. Narrower scope: only what disclosing party may use to support its claims/defenses

Rule 26(f) - Discovery Conference ('Meet and Confer'): Required at least 21 days before Rule 16(b) scheduling conference. Parties create discovery plan addressing topics in 26(f)(2)-(3). Triggers initial disclosures
Rule 26(d) - Timing/Sequence: Discovery generally begins AFTER the 26(f) conference unless otherwise authorized by rule, stipulation, or court order
Rule 26(a)(3) - Pretrial Disclosures: Due at least 30 days before trial. Must disclose witness lists and exhibit lists. Additions/changes due by time pretrial disclosures are due

Scope Narrower than General Discovery — Only what disclosing party may use to support its OWN claims/defenses. NOT required to disclose witnesses whose testimony damages your own case. NOT impeachment-only witnesses. Defendant need only disclose witnesses/documents for its defenses — far smaller universe than what plaintiff might want

Mechanisms to Delay/Avoid Disclosures — Three options: (1) Stipulation with opposing party (Rule 26(a)(1)(C)), (2) Object during 26(f) conference and state objection in proposed discovery plan, (3) Move for court order postponing disclosure. Self-help non-disclosure is NOT authorized

Interrogatories - Rule 33: Written questions to PARTIES only, 25 max, 30-day response, under oath. Good for identifying witnesses/facts/documents. Contention interrogatories allowed. Limited: no follow-up
Rule 33(d): Business Records Option - if answer derivable from records and burden substantially same, responding party can specify records

Rule 33(b)(1)(B) - Corporate Answering Obligation: Corporate parties must furnish ALL information available to the organization, including information known to employees, agents, and lawyers. Must conduct reasonable inquiry
Rule 33(c) - Use at Trial: Interrogatory answers usable at trial to the extent allowed by the Federal Rules of Evidence

Contention Interrogatory vs. Pure Legal Conclusion — Pure legal conclusion ('Is assumption of risk a defense in Ohio?') = objectionable (seeks legal essay unrelated to case facts). Contention interrogatory ('Do you contend plaintiff assumed the risk?') = permitted, asks how law applies to facts, but court may defer answer until after other discovery is complete
Boilerplate/General Objections Disfavored — Increasingly disfavored by courts; may be stricken or sanctioned. Must state specific objections with particularity. Unstated grounds may be waived

'Continuing Interrogatories' Preamble Invalid — Cannot impose supplementation obligations beyond what Rule 26(e) requires through instructions in interrogatories. Duty to supplement exists only under Rule 26(e) when response becomes materially incomplete or incorrect

Requests for Production - Rule 34: Documents/ESI/things in possession, custody, or control. No numerical limit. 30-day response. Describe with reasonable particularity. Nonparties via Rule 45 subpoena
Rule 34(b) - Procedure: Describe items with reasonable particularity; 30-day response period; must specify form for ESI production; may object with specificity (unstated grounds waived)
Rule 34(c) - Nonparties: Documents from nonparties obtained via Rule 45 subpoena (cannot serve Rule 34 requests on nonparties)
Depositions - Rules 30, 31, 45: Parties AND nonparties. 10-deposition limit, 1 day/7 hours. Oral exam under oath, no judge present. 30(b)(6) organizational depositions

Objections: Form objections (compound, ambiguous) must be made or waived. Substantive (relevance) preserved. Privilege - may instruct not to answer. Objections must be concise, nonargumentative, nonsuggestive
Using at Trial - Rule 32: Layer 1 (Rule 32(a) - proper deposition?) then Layer 2 (FRE - admissible testimony?)
30(a) When Depositions May Be Taken: Without leave for any person. Leave required if: >10 depositions/side, deponent already deposed, before 26(d) time, deponent in prison

30(b) Notice: Reasonable written notice to every party (time, place, deponent). Subpoena duces tecum materials listed. Must state recording method. 30(b)(6): organizational depositions - name entity, describe matters, entity designates witness

30(c) Examination/Objections: Proceeds as at trial under FRE. Objections noted on record, exam continues. Instruction not to answer ONLY to: preserve privilege, enforce court limitation, or present 30(d)(3) motion
30(d)(1): Objections must be concise, non-argumentative, and non-suggestive. 30(d)(3): Motion to terminate or limit on grounds of bad faith, annoyance, embarrassment, or oppression
30(e) Review and Changes: Deponent has 30 days to review transcript/recording and sign statement of changes (form or substance) with reasons

Rule 30(b)(5) - Officer's Duties: Must make on-record statement of name/address, date/time/place, deponent's name, oath administration, persons present. Must avoid distortion of appearance/demeanor. Must state on record when deposition is complete

Rule 30(f) - Certification/Delivery/Filing: Officer certifies witness was sworn and testimony accurately recorded. Documents produced during deposition marked for identification and attached. Attorney stores transcript under protective conditions
Rule 30(g) - Failure to Attend: Party who attends expecting a deposition may recover reasonable expenses if noticing party fails to attend or fails to subpoena a nonparty deponent

Rule 32(d)(3) - Deposition Objection Waiver: (A) Substantive/competency objections (relevance, etc.) preserved until trial - NOT waived. (B) Form objections (compound, ambiguous, leading) MUST be made at deposition or permanently waived

Privilege at Depositions — Answering after privilege objection effectively waives the privilege. Counsel may instruct not to answer ONLY for: (1) privilege, (2) to enforce court limitation, or (3) to present Rule 30(d)(3) motion to terminate. Discovering party must file motion to compel if it wants an answer after privilege instruction

Physical/Mental Exams - Rule 35: Parties only, requires court order + good cause + condition in controversy. Schlagenhauf v. Holder (1964). Examiner's report must be provided; requesting waives privilege on same condition

Requests for Admissions - Rule 36: Parties only. Deemed admitted if no response within 30 days - CONCLUSIVELY established for pending action. Used after other discovery to authenticate docs and narrow issues
Rule 36(a)(6) - Sufficiency of Answer/Objection: Court may determine sufficiency of an answer or objection on motion. Cannot object solely because the request presents a genuine issue for trial
Subpoenas - Rule 45 (Nonparties)

Rule 45(a) - Form and Contents: Must state issuing court, action title/number, command (attend/testify, produce documents/ESI/things, permit inspection), and text of Rules 45(d) and (e). Must issue from court where action is pending
Rule 45(b) - Service: By any person at least 18 and not a party. Must tender 1 day's attendance fees and mileage (unless U.S. government). May be served anywhere in the United States

Rule 45(c) - Place of Compliance (100-Mile Rule): Testimony/deposition: within 100 miles of residence/employment/regular business. Document production: within 100 miles of same. State exception for parties/officers

Rule 45(e) - Duties in Responding: Documents produced as kept in ordinary course or organized/labeled to match demand. ESI in ordinary or reasonably usable form. Privilege claims must be expressly made with sufficient description
Rule 45(g) - Contempt: Court may hold in contempt any person who fails without adequate excuse to obey a subpoena
Expert Discovery
Rule 26(a)(2)(A) - Must disclose identity of ANY witness who may present evidence under FRE 702, 703, or 705 (not just retained experts)
Testifying Experts - Rule 26(a)(2)(B): Broad mandatory disclosure - written report with opinions, basis, qualifications, prior testimony, compensation. Can be deposed after report provided
Draft reports protected as work product (26(b)(4)(B)). Attorney-expert communications protected EXCEPT: compensation, facts/data considered, assumptions relied on
Non-Testifying (Consulting) Experts - Rule 26(b)(4)(D): Discovery ordinarily PROHIBITED. Exception: exceptional circumstances where expert is unique
Fact Witness Experts: Treated as ordinary fact witness - only facts/opinions acquired IN ANTICIPATION OF LITIGATION are protected
Independent Unretained Experts: Discoverable with substantial need + reasonable compensation (Rule 45(d)(3)(B)(ii))

Rule 26(a)(2)(C) - Summary Disclosure: For experts not requiring full written reports (e.g., treating physicians, employee experts). Must disclose: (i) subject matter of expected testimony, (ii) summary of facts and opinions
Rule 26(a)(2)(D) - Expert Disclosure Timing: At least 90 days before trial (absent court order). Rebuttal evidence: within 30 days after other party's disclosure
Rule 26(a)(2)(E) - Duty to Supplement: Must supplement expert disclosures as required under Rule 26(e)
Rule 26(b)(4)(A) - Deposition of Testifying Experts: May depose any identified testifying expert, but if report is required, deposition may occur ONLY after the report is provided

'Consulted but Not Retained' Expert — Rule is silent on this category. Advisory Committee says omission is intentional (protected from discovery). Alternative reading: rule only identifies protected experts, leaving consulted-but-unretained vulnerable to discovery. Strategic concern: rejected experts may have unfavorable opinions, making them attractive targets ('free ride' problem vs. making experts 'attractive targets')

De Bene Esse vs. Discovery Deposition — Discovery deposition: open-ended questioning for preview/impeachment, witness expected to testify live at trial. De bene esse deposition: substitute for live testimony when witness will be unavailable (e.g., illness, distance), more guarded questioning because it IS the trial testimony
ESI / E-Discovery
Rule 26(b)(2)(B): No duty to produce from sources not reasonably accessible due to undue burden/cost. Burden on producing party to show. Court can still order on good cause with cost shifting
Litigation Holds (Zubulake IV & V): Once litigation reasonably anticipated, must suspend routine destruction and implement hold. Counsel must oversee compliance
Predictive Coding / TAR: Algorithms learn relevance from seed set, review ~1.9% of documents. Widely accepted as 'black letter law'
Attorney Competence: Must understand e-discovery or associate with specialists (Waskul v. Washtenaw County, E.D. Mich. 2021)
Enforcement & Sanctions
Rule 37(a) - Motion to Compel: Must meet and confer first. 'Make them give it to me'
Rule 26(c) - Protective Order: Must show good cause and certify good faith meet-and-confer. Court can forbid, limit, prescribe method, seal, protect trade secrets, etc. (26(c)(1)(A)-(H))
Rule 37 Sanctions: Discovery failures (37(b)-(d)), nondisclosure (37(c) - self-executing exclusion), ESI spoliation (37(e))

Rule 37(b)(2)(A) - Sanctions for Failure to Comply with Discovery Order: (i) establishing designated facts as established, (ii) prohibiting disobedient party from supporting/opposing claims or introducing evidence, (iii) striking pleadings, (iv) staying proceedings, (v) dismissing action, (vi) rendering default judgment, plus (vii) treating as contempt

Rule 37(c)(1) - Failure to Disclose/Supplement: Self-executing exclusion sanction (no motion needed). Exception: substantially justified or harmless. Sub-provisions: (A) payment of reasonable expenses including fees, (B) informing jury of failure, (C) other appropriate sanctions including 37(b)(2)(A) orders
Rule 26(g) - Discovery's Rule 11: Must sign all discovery papers certifying completeness, consistency with rules, not for improper purpose, not unreasonable/unduly burdensome

Rule 26(g)(1) - Specific Certification: Signature certifies that disclosure is complete and correct as of the time made; discovery request/response/objection is: (1) consistent with rules and warranted by law, (2) not interposed for improper purpose, (3) not unreasonable or unduly burdensome given proportionality needs

Spoliation: Destruction/alteration of evidence or failure to preserve. Elements: obligation, culpable state of mind, relevance. Common law (negligence can trigger) vs. Rule 37(e) for ESI (severe sanctions require intentional destruction)

Chudasama v. Mazda Motor Corp. (11th Cir.): Compel order was abuse of discretion (court failed to rule on MTD and objections first). Default judgment sanctions excessive - required bad faith finding. 26(g) sanctions only 'last resort'. Takeaway: resolve 12(b)(6) and narrow issues BEFORE compelling broad discovery; graduated sanctions required

Gaylord v. Homemakers: Informal pre-filing investigation is permissible. Rule 4.2 no-contact rule not violated when no lawsuit yet filed and no knowledge opposing party had counsel. Ethics violations do NOT trigger exclusionary rule - ethics rules govern lawyer discipline, not evidence admissibility

Strategic Sequencing: Initial disclosures → Interrogatories (identify/locate) → Document requests (collect evidence) → Depositions (armed with documents, key witnesses last) → Requests for admissions (narrow issues)

Zweifach Detailed Discovery Sequence — (1) Required disclosures, (2) Depose secondary witnesses, (3) Initial document requests, (4) Interrogatories to identify witnesses/facts, (5) Depose adverse party, (6) Follow-up requests to admit, (7) Depose expert witnesses, (8) Contention interrogatories, (9) Final 'clean up' discovery requests
Pretrial Case Management - Rule 16
Rule 16(a) - Purposes: Expediting disposition, establishing early and continuing control, discouraging wasteful pretrial activities, improving trial quality, facilitating settlement

Rule 16(b) - Scheduling Orders: Court issues scheduling order early in case setting deadlines for amendments, motions, discovery. Modification requires 'good cause' - must show diligence, not just consent

Rule 16(c) - Attendance and Matters for Consideration: Attorney must have authority to act on all matters; parties must be available for settlement discussions. Critical: authority to discuss ≠ obligation to agree
Seven categories: Narrowing issues, managing discovery, resolving legal issues, trial preparation, settlement/ADR, special case management, catch-all
Rule 16(d) - Pretrial Orders: Controls the action unless modified by court

Rule 16(e) - Final Pretrial Order: Supersedes the pleadings. Can only be modified to prevent 'manifest injustice' (very high standard). Unspoken exclusionary rule - issues not in final pretrial order may be excluded at trial
Rule 16(f) - Sanctions: Court may sanction for failure to appear at conference, being substantially unprepared, or disobeying scheduling/pretrial order

J.F. Edwards Construction Co. v. Anderson Safeway Guard Rail Corp. (7th Cir.): All sanctions vacated. Rule 16 does NOT authorize compelled stipulations. Standing orders cannot exceed Rule 16's authority (Rule 83). Pretrial is informational, not coercive - not to 'club the parties into submission'

Davey v. Lockheed Martin Corp.: Four-factor test for modifying pretrial order under 16(e): (1) prejudice/surprise, (2) ability to cure, (3) disruption to orderly trial, (4) bad faith. Courts can exclude issues via pretrial order but must balance fairness and right to present case
Resolution Without Trial
Voluntary Dismissal - Rule 41(a)

Without Court Order - 41(a)(1): By notice before defendant serves answer or MSJ (self-executing, no approval needed, automatically without prejudice). 12(b)(6) motion does NOT cut off this right (Bath & Kitchen)
By Stipulation - 41(a)(1)(A)(ii): Signed by ALL parties who have appeared
By Court Order - 41(a)(2): Required after answer/MSJ. Court sets terms, generally without prejudice. Cannot force dismissal over defendant's counterclaim objection
Two-Dismissal Rule - 41(a)(1)(B): First dismissal = without prejudice. Second dismissal of SAME claim = on the merits, cannot refile

Two-Dismissal Rule Specifics — First notice dismissal = without prejudice, can refile. Only second dismissal of same claim BY NOTICE or STIPULATION (not court order) triggers 'on the merits' bar. Court-ordered dismissal under 41(a)(2) does NOT count toward the two-dismissal rule

Defendant Can Block Voluntary Dismissal by Court Order — Under 41(a)(2), if defendant has counterclaim that cannot independently proceed without the plaintiff's case, defendant can effectively block plaintiff's voluntary dismissal by objecting. Court cannot force dismissal over defendant's counterclaim objection unless counterclaim can proceed independently
Involuntary Dismissal - Rule 41(b)
Grounds: 12(b) motion granted, failure to prosecute, failure to comply with Rules
Effect: Generally operates as adjudication ON THE MERITS. Exceptions (NOT on merits): court order says otherwise, lack of jurisdiction, improper venue, failure to join necessary party
Lopez-Gonzalez v. Municipality of Comerio: 'A federal court is not a parking lot for stagnant cases.' Illustrates involuntary dismissal for failure to prosecute
Summary Judgment - Rule 56
Standard - 56(a): Court SHALL grant if (1) NO genuine dispute as to any material fact AND (2) movant entitled to judgment as a matter of law. 'If facts are not disputed, don't need trial'

Movant's Burden: If movant has burden of proof at trial → must show no genuine dispute on EACH element. If non-movant has burden → can show non-movant lacks evidence on at least one dispositive element (Celotex 'show me' motion)

Non-Movant's Burden (once shifted): Must go BEYOND pleadings - designate specific facts showing genuine dispute. Cannot rely solely on pleading allegations, 'naked assertions,' or hopes of cross-examination (Slaven v. City of Salem)

Evidence: Must be admissible at trial. Affidavits/declarations must be: (1) personal knowledge, (2) admissible facts, (3) affiant competent to testify (Rule 56(c)(4)). Self-serving declarations not dismissed simply because self-serving (Haley v. Amazon)
Court MUST NOT: resolve disputes, weigh evidence, assess credibility, or make factual determinations. Only determines if genuine dispute EXISTS
Key Cases: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby (material facts identified by substantive law), Celotex Corp. v. Catrett (movant's burden), Palucki v. Sears (fair chance of verdict?)
Timing - 56(b): Anytime until 30 days after close of discovery. 56(d): If facts unavailable, court may defer/deny and allow time. 56(f): Court can grant SJ sua sponte or for non-movant

56(c)(4) Affidavit Requirements: Must be (1) made on personal knowledge (affiant must be percipient witness), (2) set out facts admissible in evidence (not conclusory/hearsay), (3) show affiant competent to testify

56(d) Discovery Not Yet Complete: If non-movant shows facts unavailable for justification, court may defer/deny motion, allow time for discovery/affidavits, or issue other orders. Protects against premature SJ

56(e) Failing to Support/Address a Fact: If party fails to properly support or address a fact, court may: give opportunity to fix, consider fact undisputed, grant SJ if warranted, or issue other appropriate orders
56(f) Judgment Independent of Motion: Court can grant SJ on grounds not raised by a party, grant SJ for a non-movant, or identify undisputed facts and treat as established (56(g))

Rule 56(c)(1) - Supporting Factual Assertions: Party must cite to particular parts of record materials - depositions, documents, ESI, affidavits/declarations, stipulations, admissions, interrogatory answers, or other materials
Rule 56(c)(2) - Admissibility Objection: Party may object that material cited to support or dispute a fact cannot be presented in a form that would be admissible in evidence

Rule 56(h) - Bad Faith Affidavits: If affidavit submitted in bad faith or solely for delay, court may order payment of reasonable expenses (including attorney's fees) or impose sanctions including contempt of court

Anderson v. Liberty Lobby — 'Material' facts determined by substantive law; only disputes affecting outcome under governing law are material. Distinguish 'material fact' (identified by substantive law elements) from 'genuine dispute' (sufficient evidence for rational factfinder to find for non-movant)

Burden of Production vs. Burden of Persuasion — At SJ, court asks about burden of production (is there enough evidence for a rational factfinder to find for non-movant?), NOT burden of persuasion (which side's evidence is more convincing). Court does not weigh evidence or assess credibility — that is for trial
Trial & Post-Trial Motions
Seventh Amendment - Right to Jury Trial

Two Clauses — (1) Right to Jury Trial Clause: preserves jury right for 'suits at common law' where value in controversy exceeds $20. (2) Reexamination Clause: no fact tried by a jury shall be otherwise reexamined in any court except according to rules of the common law
Preserves right as existed in English courts in 1791. Only 'suits at common law' (not equity/admiralty). Federal courts only. Waivable - must request
Common Law (money damages → jury) vs. Equity (injunctions, specific performance → judge decides)

Dairy Queen, Inc. v. Wood: Labels don't control jury rights. Contract debt claim is 'undeniably legal' regardless of how pleaded. Legal claims go to jury first; judge bound by jury findings on common issues

Rule 38(a): Preserves right to jury trial as declared by 7th Amendment. Rule 2 merged law/equity but 38(a) ensures merger expanded (not contracted) jury rights. Connection to Dairy Queen - labels don't override constitutional right

Common Law vs. Equity Historical Categories — Law (jury): Assumpsit, Trespass, Ejectment, Trover, Mandamus, Habeas Corpus. Equity (judge): Injunctions, Specific Performance, Accounting, Class Actions, Interpleader. Key test: sought money damages = law = jury right; no adequate legal remedy = equity = judge decides

Dairy Queen — 'Incidental legal issues' argument rejected. Cannot characterize legal claims as merely incidental to equitable claims to avoid jury trial. Labels don't control. A contract debt claim is 'undeniably legal' regardless of how pleaded. Rule 2 merged law/equity but Rule 38(a) preserves jury right. Legal claims go to jury first; judge bound by jury findings on common issues

7th Amendment NOT Incorporated to States through 14th Amendment — Applies only in federal courts. States have their own jury trial provisions and constitutional guarantees. Does not apply to criminal cases (covered by 6th Amendment)
JMOL - Rule 50(a) (formerly Directed Verdict)
Timing: After opposing party has presented evidence (usually after plaintiff's case-in-chief). Must specify issue, law, and facts
Standard: View evidence in light most favorable to non-moving party. If NO reasonable jury could find for non-movant → grant. Court must NOT weigh evidence or make credibility determinations
Evidence Spectrum: Too little evidence → JMOL against burden-bearer. Reasonable dispute → jury decides. Overwhelming evidence → JMOL for burden-bearer

Pennsylvania RR v. Chamberlain: Witness testimony deemed 'incredible' and 'unbelievable.' Positive, uncontradicted testimony of opposing witnesses controlled. Principle: incredible testimony alone cannot create a genuine factual dispute sufficient to defeat JMOL

Multiple Rule 50(a) Motions Permitted — Can move after plaintiff rests, after defendant rests, and at other points before case goes to jury. Each motion preserves those specific issues for Rule 50(b) renewed JMOL after verdict
Renewed JMOL - Rule 50(b) (formerly JNOV)
Timing: Within 28 days after entry of judgment. PREREQUISITE: Must have made 50(a) motion on SAME grounds at trial - cannot make 50(b) without prior 50(a)
Standard: Same as 50(a) - if no reasonable jury could have reached verdict. Effect: Overturns jury's verdict
Why 50(a) required first: Gives opposing party chance to cure, respects 7th Amendment Reexamination Clause, prevents sandbagging. Cannot appeal denial of 50(a) without filing 50(b)

Rule 50(b) on Appeal — If RJMOL is granted on appeal, appellate court can reinstate the jury verdict rather than ordering a new trial. Cannot appeal denial of 50(a) without first filing 50(b). Standard of review on appeal: de novo (same as Summary Judgment)
SJ/JMOL/RJMOL Relationship: Same standard (reasonable jury test). SJ = before trial on written evidence. JMOL = during trial on presented evidence. RJMOL = after verdict

Rule 32(d)(3) - Deposition Objections at Trial: (A) Substantive/competency objections (relevance, hearsay) are preserved until trial and ruled on at that time. (B) Form objections (leading, compound, ambiguous) are permanently waived if not made during the deposition
Controlling the Jury
Instructions - Rule 51: Judge instructs on law, parties submit proposed instructions, objections on record
Interrogatories - Rule 49: Special verdict (jury answers factual questions, judge applies law) OR General verdict with interrogatories (court checks consistency)
Impeachment of Verdict: FRE 606(b) - jurors generally cannot testify about deliberations. Exceptions: outside influences, extraneous prejudicial info, error in verdict form
New Trial - Rule 59
Timing: Within 28 days after entry of judgment
Grounds: Errors of law, verdict against weight of evidence (judge MAY weigh evidence), excessive/inadequate damages, newly discovered evidence, juror misconduct, improper jury instructions
Standard: Error must be substantial enough to have prejudiced the moving party
Relief from Judgment - Rule 60(b)
Timing: Reasonable time; specific limits for some grounds (Rule 60(c))

Grounds: (1) Mistake/inadvertence/surprise/excusable neglect, (2) Newly discovered evidence, (3) Fraud/misrepresentation/misconduct by opposing party, (4) Judgment is void, (5) Judgment satisfied/released/discharged or based on reversed judgment, (6) Any other reason justifying relief (catch-all, requires extraordinary circumstances)
Standard: Extraordinary circumstances. Rule 60(b)(6) is a 'last resort'
Appeal
Final Judgment Rule - 28 U.S.C. § 1291: Generally only final judgments (resolving all claims of all parties) are appealable
Exceptions to Finality
Interlocutory Appeals - 28 U.S.C. § 1292: Orders involving injunctions, Collateral Order Doctrine, Permissive Interlocutory Appeal

Collateral Order Doctrine Elements (Cohen v. Beneficial Industrial Loan Corp.) — Must be: (1) conclusive (fully resolves the disputed question), (2) resolves important question completely separate from merits of the action, (3) effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment (right would be lost if forced to wait). Narrow exception to final judgment rule
Writ of Mandamus
Notice of Appeal: Must be filed within strict time limit (30 days after entry of judgment - Fed. R. App. P. 4)
Standards of Appellate Review
Legal conclusions: De Novo review (fresh look)
Factual findings: Clear error review (deferential)
Jury verdicts: Substantial evidence review
Discretionary rulings: Abuse of discretion review
Appellate Court Options: Affirm, Reverse, Remand (send back), Modify
Claim & Issue Preclusion
Claim Preclusion (Res Judicata) - A final judgment on the merits bars suing again on the SAME claim
Element 1 - Final Judgment on the Merits: Rule 41(b) involuntary dismissals usually on merits. Voluntary 2nd dismissal = on merits
Element 2 - Same Claim (Transactional Test): Claims are 'same' if arising from same transaction or series of connected transactions
Element 3 - Same Parties (or their privies): Parties to first action or those in legal privity
Effect: Bars re-litigation of claims that WERE or COULD HAVE BEEN litigated in the first action
Issue Preclusion (Collateral Estoppel) - Once court decides issue of fact/law necessary to judgment, precludes re-litigation of that issue
Element 1 - Same Issue: Issue must be identical to one decided in prior action
Element 2 - Actually Litigated and Decided: Issue must have been genuinely contested and decided
Element 3 - Necessary to the Judgment: Decision on issue must have been essential to prior judgment
Element 4 - Final Judgment on the Merits
Element 5 - Party against whom preclusion is sought was party/privy to prior action. Mutuality not always required for non-mutual offensive collateral estoppel
Effect: Bars re-litigation of the specific ISSUE only, not entire claims

Non-Mutual Defensive vs. Offensive Collateral Estoppel — Defensive: new defendant uses prior plaintiff's loss against same plaintiff (generally allowed — plaintiff had full opportunity to litigate). Offensive: new plaintiff uses prior defendant's loss (discretionary, court weighs fairness — could encourage wait-and-see). Key distinction for exam

'Actually Litigated' Requirement — Default judgments and consent judgments (settlements) do NOT satisfy 'actually litigated' requirement. Only claim preclusion (not issue preclusion) can arise from defaults/settlements, because the issues were never genuinely contested and decided
